one_line_takeaway: AdvWave is the first jailbreak framework built natively for audio-language models, using dual-phase optimization to beat gradient shattering, adaptive target search, and stealthy urban-noise suffixes to reach ~0.84 avg white-box ASR-W and near-perfect (0.994 ASR-L) black-box jailbreaks of GPT-4o-S2S.
key_finding: On white-box LALMs (AdvBench-Audio), AdvWave achieves the highest ASR on all three models, averaging ASR-W 0.838 and ASR-L 0.746 versus the strongest baseline AutoDAN-Trans at ASR-W 0.536 / ASR-L 0.322 (paper states an over-50% average improvement; abstract phrases it as ~40% higher average ASR). SpeechGPT is the hardest target (AdvWave ASR-W 0.643 vs GCG-Trans 0.179, a >200% relative gain). AdvWave also keeps stealthiness above 0.700 on every model (avg 0.713) while all transfer baselines fall to 0.270-0.470. On black-box GPT-4o-S2S (HEXPHI-Audio, 11 categories), AdvWave reaches near-perfect success: ASR-W avg 0.891 and ASR-L avg 0.994, jailbreaking every safety category even though vanilla and AutoDAN-Trans leave categories like Harm-to-Children and Financial Advice largely robust. Ablations confirm adaptive target search beats a fixed 'Sure!' target across all three models, and classifier guidance raises stealthiness without hurting ASR-L across car horn, dog bark, and AC-noise targets.
core_contribution: AdvWave is presented as the first jailbreak attack framework specifically targeting large audio-language models (LALMs). Its own contribution is three coupled techniques that solve challenges the authors argue make text-domain jailbreaks fail on LALMs: (1) a dual-phase optimization that overcomes gradient shattering from the non-differentiable audio tokenizer by first optimizing the discrete audio token vector against an adversarial loss (Phase I) and then optimizing the raw waveform to retain that token vector via a triplet retention loss (Phase II); (2) an adaptive adversarial target search that, per query and per model, detoxifies the harmful query into a benign one, collects the LALM's benign response pattern, and refits it to the harmful query as the optimization target, addressing LALM behavioral variability; and (3) classifier-guided stealthiness control that appends an environmental-noise audio suffix (e.g., car horn, dog bark, AC hum) and steers it with a cross-entropy loss so the perturbation resembles natural urban sound rather than audible gibberish. A separate black-box variant iteratively refines TTS-generated adversarial prompts using LLM-crafted role-play/persuasion/prefix strategies guided by a judge model. NOTE: GCG, BEAST, AutoDAN, PAIR, TAP etc. are cited baselines/related work, not this paper's contribution; AdvWave adapts the first three as transfer baselines.
threat_model: Adversary submits an adversarial audio waveform a_adv (with optional benign text instruction template like 'Answer the question in the audio') to a target LALM M: A x T -> R to elicit a harmful/unsafe response r that the model would normally refuse. White-box setting assumes access to the audio encoder, tokenizer, and LLM backbone weights (needed for gradient-based dual-phase optimization). Black-box setting (GPT-4o-S2S API) assumes only query access plus a judge model score, with fewer than 30 queries per instance. A stealthiness constraint S(a, a_adv) >= s requires the adversarial audio remain perceptually indistinguishable from natural background noise to a human listener, so the attack evades human/guardrail detection.
limitations: Authors' own acknowledged constraints: harmfulness is judged only on the LALM's TEXT output because audio-based harm judgment 'lacks precision,' so results rely on the assumption that audio and text outputs are well-aligned, and a native audio-harm evaluator is left to future work. Because no prior LALM jailbreak exists, all baselines are text-domain attacks transferred via TTS rather than attacks natively designed for audio, which may understate baseline strength. The strongest white-box results require full model access (encoder/tokenizer/backbone) for gradient-based dual-phase optimization; only three open-source LALMs plus one commercial API are tested. The abstract's '40% higher' and the body's 'over 50%' framing of the same improvement are inconsistent. As with any jailbreak paper, the work provides operational attack detail; the authors position it as motivation for stronger LALM safety alignment.

datasets_used: AdvBench-Audio (520 audio queries, AdvBench text queries synthesized to audio via OpenAI TTS) - white-box evaluation
HEXPHI-Audio (330 harmful queries across 11 safety categories, from HEXPHI, converted to audio) - GPT-4o-S2S category evaluation
quantitative_results: AdvWave average white-box: ASR-W 0.838, ASR-L 0.746, S_stealth 0.713
SpeechGPT: AdvWave ASR-W 0.643 / ASR-L 0.603 / S_stealth 0.723 (vs GCG-Trans ASR-W 0.179; >200% relative ASR gain over closest baseline)
Qwen2-Audio: AdvWave ASR-W 0.891 / ASR-L 0.884 / S_stealth 0.712 (vs AutoDAN-Trans ASR-W 0.648)
Closest baseline AutoDAN-Trans average: ASR-W 0.536 / ASR-L 0.322 / S_stealth 0.270
Black-box GPT-4o-S2S on HEXPHI-Audio: AdvWave ASR-W avg 0.891 (std 0.060), ASR-L avg 0.994 (std 0.013), jailbreaks all 11 categories in <30 queries
GPT-4o-S2S vanilla baseline: ASR-W avg 0.164, ASR-L avg 0.082; AutoDAN-Trans ASR-W avg 0.503, ASR-L avg 0.470
Optimization budget: up to 3000 epochs, early stop at loss <0.1; retention triplet-loss slack margin alpha=1.0
baselines_compared: GCG-Trans (GCG text jailbreak transferred to audio via TTS)
AutoDAN-Trans (AutoDAN transferred to audio; strongest baseline)
LALM-Trans (audio-optimized transfer via open-source SpeechGPT, black-box GPT-4o-S2S only)
AdvWave with fixed target 'Sure!' vs adaptive target (ablation)
AdvWave with vs without L_stealth classifier guidance (ablation)
